breaking_news.title: Handala Hacktivist Group Wipes 200K+ Systems in Iran-Backed Attack on Stryker
breaking_news.summary:
Paragraph 1: The Iran-linked hacktivist group Handala claimed responsibility for a destructive wiper malware attack against global medical technology company Stryker, stating it erased data from over 200,000 systems, servers, and mobile devices across 79 countries. The group, which has ties to Iran’s intelligence agencies, posted a manifesto on Telegram asserting the attack was retaliation for U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran. Stryker confirmed the incident, with reports of employees being sent home and internal systems disrupted.

Paragraph 2: The attack has severely impacted Stryker’s global operations, including its largest hub in Ireland where over 5,000 workers were sent home. Given Stryker’s critical role in supplying life-saving medical devices—including orthopedic implants and surgical equipment—the disruption poses direct risks to patient care and hospital operations worldwide. This incident underscores the growing threat of politically motivated wiper attacks targeting critical healthcare infrastructure.

breaking_news.title: UNC6426 Leaks Stolen GitHub Tokens to Compromise AWS Environments in 72 Hours
breaking_news.summary:
Paragraph 1: The threat actor UNC6426 exploited previously stolen GitHub tokens from the nx npm package supply chain compromise to breach cloud environments in under 72 hours. Using the compromised tokens, the group gained unauthorized access to cloud infrastructure, escalated privileges, and exfiltrated sensitive data. This attack demonstrates how supply chain compromises in developer tooling can rapidly cascade into full cloud account takeovers when developers reuse or improperly store tokens.

Paragraph 2: The incident highlights the severe risk posed by supply chain attacks targeting developer ecosystems like npm and GitHub. Organizations relying on CI/CD pipelines and cloud infrastructure are especially vulnerable, as stolen tokens can bypass traditional authentication and enable lateral movement across cloud services. This case reinforces the need for token rotation, least-privilege access controls, and continuous monitoring of developer tool integrations.

breaking_news.title: ShinyHunters Abuses AuraInspector Tool to Mass-Scan Salesforce for Data Theft
breaking_news.summary:
Paragraph 1: Threat actors, including the ShinyHunters group, are mass-scanning Salesforce Experience Cloud sites using a modified version of the open-source AuraInspector tool to exploit misconfigured guest user permissions. By leveraging overly permissive settings, attackers harvest sensitive data—including Accounts, Contacts, and Leads—without authentication. Salesforce has confirmed this campaign is widespread, with ShinyHunters claiming to have stolen data from ~400 high-profile organizations, including Salesforce itself.

Paragraph 2: This attack highlights the growing trend of attackers repurposing legitimate security tools for malicious use, combined with misconfiguration abuse to bypass traditional perimeter defenses. With Salesforce deployments ubiquitous across enterprises, this campaign poses massive exposure risk to any organization using Experience Cloud without rigorous access control audits. The incident underscores the critical need for continuous configuration validation and real-time monitoring of guest user permissions.

breaking_news.title: CISA Orders Emergency Patching of n8n RCE Flaw Actively Exploited in Wild
breaking_news.summary:
Paragraph 1: CISA issued an emergency directive requiring all U.S. federal agencies to patch CVE-2024-29415, a critical remote code execution (RCE) vulnerability in the n8n workflow automation platform, due to confirmed active exploitation in attacks. The flaw allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary code, potentially leading to full system compromise. The vulnerability was patched in n8n versions released in early March, but widespread unpatched deployments remain a concern.

Paragraph 2: n8n is widely used across enterprises for automating workflows between SaaS applications, making this vulnerability especially dangerous. Successful exploitation could enable attackers to pivot laterally, steal credentials, or exfiltrate data from connected systems. The CISA directive reflects the severity of the threat and signals that organizations outside the federal sector should treat this as an urgent patching priority.

breaking_news.title: BlackSanta Malware Targets HR with EDR-Killing Capabilities
breaking_news.summary:
Paragraph 1: A Russian-speaking threat actor is deploying the BlackSanta malware via fake job applications sent to HR departments. The malware includes an EDR and antivirus killer component that disables endpoint protections at the kernel level before executing credential theft, system reconnaissance, and data exfiltration. The attack chain typically begins with an ISO file disguised as a CV, tricking HR staff into launching the malicious payload.

Paragraph 2: This campaign demonstrates the increasing sophistication of social engineering targeting non-IT departments, coupled with advanced anti-defense capabilities. By disabling security tools before detonating payloads, attackers significantly increase their dwell time and success rate. HR departments—often lacking robust endpoint security awareness—are especially vulnerable, making this a high-impact supply chain–adjacent threat to enterprise security posture.

breaking_news.title: APT28 Deploys BEARDSHELL and COVENANT Malware in Long-Term Espionage on Ukrainian Forces
breaking_news.summary:
Paragraph 1: APT28 (Fancy Bear), a Russia-linked advanced persistent threat group, has been conducting long-term espionage against Ukrainian military personnel since April 2024 using two custom implants: BEARDSHELL and COVENANT. These implants, built on code lineage tracing back to the group’s 2010-era tooling, enable persistent surveillance, data collection, and resilience via dual-cloud infrastructure. ESET researchers confirmed the campaign’s activity through telemetry and forensic analysis.

Paragraph 2: The sustained nature of this operation—spanning over a year—highlights APT28’s continued focus on Ukrainian and NATO-affiliated targets amid ongoing geopolitical tensions. The use of custom implants and dual-cloud redundancy demonstrates advanced operational security and persistence capabilities. This campaign serves as a stark reminder that state-sponsored actors maintain persistent access in high-priority conflict zones, often operating beneath detection thresholds for extended periods.

breaking_news.title: KadNap Botnet Infects 14,000+ ASUS Routers to Route Malicious Traffic
breaking_news.summary:
Paragraph 1: The KadNap malware has infected over 14,000 edge devices—primarily ASUS routers—turning them into a stealth proxy botnet used to route malicious traffic. First detected in August 2025, the campaign has expanded globally, with over 60% of infections located in the U.S. The malware uses a peer-to-peer Kademlia-based protocol to hide command infrastructure and routes traffic through a proxy service called Doppelganger, likely linked to the Faceless network and TheMoon malware.

Paragraph 2: By hijacking consumer-grade routers, attackers gain a distributed, resilient infrastructure for phishing, malware distribution, and C2 traffic obfuscation. The use of edge devices as proxies undermines traditional network security controls like firewalls and DNS filtering, as traffic appears to originate from legitimate residential networks. This campaign illustrates the growing threat of IoT botnets being repurposed for advanced cybercrime operations.

breaking_news.title: Critical HPE Aruba CX Switch Flaw Lets Attackers Reset Admin Passwords Remotely
breaking_news.summary:
Paragraph 1: A critical vulnerability (CVE-2026-23813) in HPE Aruba AOS-CX switch software allows unauthenticated remote attackers to bypass authentication and reset administrator passwords. With a CVSS score of 9.8, the flaw resides in the web-based management interface and requires no user interaction or privileges to exploit. HPE has released patches, but many production networks remain exposed due to delayed patching or management interfaces left internet-facing.

Paragraph 2: Aruba CX switches are widely deployed in enterprise and campus networks, making this flaw a high-impact risk. Successful exploitation enables attackers to gain full control of network infrastructure, facilitating lateral movement, traffic interception, or denial-of-service. This vulnerability exemplifies the dangers of exposed management interfaces and the need for network segmentation and strict access controls for critical infrastructure devices.
